response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "I've been following the news on COVID-19 and the vaccines ever since they became available, mostly online because I like staying informed through various platforms. As an elderly woman living in New York who was raised by parents without much education but worked hard—my father had a vocational diploma as a plumber's mate, my mother just finished high school—I've learned that sometimes you need to look closely at what people say rather than trusting everything outright. I think being curious is good because it helps me understand things better.\n\nWhen I hear about vaccines or any health topic these days on social media feeds like Facebook and Instagram groups where some friends post, my first reaction might be skepticism given all the conflicting information floating around online communities nowadays. But what keeps pulling me back is how much simpler life feels when you know exactly what to do—if only everyone agreed! My curiosity makes me want to dig deeper into understanding these complex subjects.\n\nI remember reading about how even highly educated people can get misinformation from biased sources they follow, and this hits home because my own parents didn't have higher education either. They taught practical skills instead. So I try not being too hard on myself when I come across confusing information online—it's just part of managing these discussions in our modern world.\n\nThere was one time recently when someone shared a post saying vaccines cause autism; I politely disagreed based on what my church teaches me about caring for others, and how much research there is supporting vaccine safety. The open personality trait within me makes me enjoy explaining things step by step like this to others whenever possible.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been following the news on COVID-19 and the vaccines ever since they became available, mostly online because I like staying informed through various platforms. As an elderly woman living in New York who was raised by parents without much education but worked hard—my father had a vocational diploma as a plumber's mate, my mother just finished high school—I've learned that sometimes you need to look closely at what people say rather than trusting everything outright. I think being curious is good because it helps me understand things better.

When I hear about vaccines or any health topic these days on social media feeds like Facebook and Instagram groups where some friends post, my first reaction might be skepticism given all the conflicting information floating around online communities nowadays. But what keeps pulling me back is how much simpler life feels when you know exactly what to do—if only everyone agreed! My curiosity makes me want to dig deeper into understanding these complex subjects.

I remember reading about how even highly educated people can get misinformation from biased sources they follow, and this hits home because my own parents didn't have higher education either. They taught practical skills instead. So I try not being too hard on myself when I come across confusing information online—it's just part of managing these discussions in our modern world.

There was one time recently when someone shared a post saying vaccines cause autism; I politely disagreed based on what my church teaches me about caring for others, and how much research there is supporting vaccine safety. The open personality trait within me makes me enjoy explaining things step by step like this to others whenever possible.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I analyzed the topic from multiple angles including personal experience with misinformation online due to daily social media use, background knowledge as a part-time employed transgender individual in New York City considering family upbringing without advanced degrees but emphasizing practical skills and work ethic. I also considered how curiosity drives me to seek clarity amidst conflicting information.
